name: Neft Teacher — Grade 6 Misconception Tags
note: Single source of truth for misconception tags used by the Thinking Trails Evidence Layer. Each tag drives student/teacher/family report language. Keep tag ids stable; they are stored in saved sessions and CSV exports.

tags.teacherDescription: Student added the values correctly but divided by a count that does not match how many numbers were in the set.
tags.suggestedTeacherMove: Have the student point to and count each value before dividing. Anchor the rule: divide by how many numbers there are.
tags.simpleFamilyLanguage: Your child added the numbers but divided by the wrong amount. Count the numbers together first.

tags.studentFriendlyName: Adding slip when finding the mean
tags.teacherDescription: Student used the correct mean procedure but made an addition error when totaling the values.
tags.suggestedTeacherMove: Slow down the sum. Have the student add in pairs and re-check the total before dividing.
tags.simpleFamilyLanguage: The steps were right, but there was a small adding mistake. Re-add the numbers slowly.

tags.studentFriendlyName: Did not put numbers in order
tags.teacherDescription: Student picked a middle value without first sorting the data set from least to greatest.
tags.suggestedTeacherMove: Reteach: order the numbers before finding the middle. Use number cards students physically rearrange.
tags.simpleFamilyLanguage: For median, always put the numbers in order first, then find the middle.

tags.teacherDescription: Student ordered the data but chose the wrong middle position (often an off-by-one, or did not average the two middles in an even set).
tags.suggestedTeacherMove: Model crossing off one number from each end until the middle remains. For even sets, average the two middle numbers.
tags.simpleFamilyLanguage: Cross off one number from each side until the middle is left.

tags.studentFriendlyName: Picked the biggest instead of the most common
tags.teacherDescription: Student confused mode with maximum and chose the largest value instead of the most frequent value.
tags.suggestedTeacherMove: Contrast 'most often' vs 'biggest'. Tally each value and circle the tallest tally column.
tags.simpleFamilyLanguage: Mode means the number that shows up the most, not the biggest number.

tags.teacherDescription: Student did not notice which value repeated, or said there was no mode when one existed.
tags.suggestedTeacherMove: Have the student make a tally or frequency table so repeats become visible.
tags.simpleFamilyLanguage: Look for the number that appears more than once — that is the mode.

tags.studentFriendlyName: Added instead of subtracting for range
tags.teacherDescription: Student added the largest and smallest values instead of subtracting to find the range.
tags.suggestedTeacherMove: Use the frame: biggest number minus smallest number. Color the high and low values, then subtract.
tags.simpleFamilyLanguage: Range is biggest minus smallest. Subtract, do not add.

tags.teacherDescription: Student tried to combine all values instead of only the maximum and minimum to find the range.
tags.suggestedTeacherMove: Reteach that range only needs two numbers: the highest and the lowest.
tags.simpleFamilyLanguage: For range you only need two numbers: the highest and the lowest.

tags.teacherDescription: Student applied the same operation instead of the inverse to isolate the variable.
tags.suggestedTeacherMove: Model the 'undo' idea: addition undoes subtraction, multiplication undoes division. Use a balance scale.
tags.simpleFamilyLanguage: To solve, do the opposite operation to get the variable alone.

tags.teacherDescription: Student performed an operation on one side of the equation but not the other, breaking the balance.
tags.suggestedTeacherMove: Reinforce 'whatever you do to one side, do to the other.' Use a two-pan balance visual.
tags.simpleFamilyLanguage: Keep the equation balanced — do the same thing to both sides.

tags.teacherDescription: Student found a value that does not satisfy the original equation when substituted back.
tags.suggestedTeacherMove: Build the habit of substituting the answer back into the original equation to verify.
tags.simpleFamilyLanguage: Put the answer back into the problem to check that it works.

tags.teacherDescription: Student made a sign or operation error (e.g., dropped a negative or used + instead of -).
tags.suggestedTeacherMove: Have the student annotate each operation and track signs explicitly down the page.
tags.simpleFamilyLanguage: Watch the plus, minus, and negative signs carefully.

tags.teacherDescription: Student used additive reasoning (adding the same amount) where multiplicative (scaling) reasoning was needed.
tags.suggestedTeacherMove: Use a ratio table and ask 'times what?' between rows rather than 'plus what?'.
tags.simpleFamilyLanguage: Ratios grow by multiplying, not by adding the same number.

tags.teacherDescription: Student attempted to scale the ratio but multiplied only one quantity or used the wrong factor.
tags.suggestedTeacherMove: Model scaling both quantities by the same factor in a ratio table.
tags.simpleFamilyLanguage: When you multiply one part of a ratio, multiply the other part by the same number.

tags.studentFriendlyName: Set up the unit rate wrong
tags.teacherDescription: Student divided in the wrong direction or paired the wrong quantities when finding a unit rate.
tags.suggestedTeacherMove: Label units before dividing (e.g., dollars per pound) so the setup matches the question.
tags.simpleFamilyLanguage: Line up the labels (like miles per hour) before you divide.

tags.teacherDescription: Student computed perimeter when area was asked, or vice versa.
tags.suggestedTeacherMove: Anchor: perimeter is the distance around (add sides); area is the space inside (square units).
tags.simpleFamilyLanguage: Perimeter is the distance around the edge. Area is the space inside.

tags.teacherDescription: Student found surface area but did not include every face of the solid.
tags.suggestedTeacherMove: Use a net so every face is visible and countable before adding.
tags.simpleFamilyLanguage: For surface area, count every side of the shape, including the ones you cannot see.

tags.studentFriendlyName: Mixed up volume and surface area
tags.teacherDescription: Student used a surface-area approach when volume was asked, or vice versa.
tags.suggestedTeacherMove: Contrast filling (volume, cubic units) vs wrapping (surface area, square units).
tags.simpleFamilyLanguage: Volume is how much fits inside. Surface area is how much wrapping covers the outside.

tags.teacherDescription: Student reached a correct or near-correct answer but explained their thinking in too few words to show reasoning.
tags.suggestedTeacherMove: Provide sentence starters and ask for at least one 'because' that names the math step.
tags.simpleFamilyLanguage: Ask your child to explain their answer in a full sentence using because.

tags.teacherDescription: Student explained an answer without using any math vocabulary (e.g., mean, median, order, divide).
tags.suggestedTeacherMove: Post a vocabulary bank and require one math word per explanation.
tags.simpleFamilyLanguage: Encourage math words like add, divide, order, or middle in the explanation.

tags.teacherDescription: Student gave only a final number with no description of how it was found.
tags.suggestedTeacherMove: Require a 'First, I... Then, I...' two-step explanation before accepting the answer.
tags.simpleFamilyLanguage: Ask 'How did you get that?' so your child shares the steps.

tags.teacherDescription: Student restated the formula or rule but did not connect it to the specific numbers in the problem.
tags.suggestedTeacherMove: Ask the student to plug the actual values into the rule and narrate each step.
tags.simpleFamilyLanguage: Ask your child to use the real numbers from the problem, not just the rule.
